data.title: Incyte and Novartis settle royalties dispute related to Jakafi
data.description:
Incyte (NASDAQ:INCY) and Novartis AG (NYSE:NVS) reached a settlement regarding royalty payments in an existing collaboration between the companies for blood-cancer treatment Jakafi.

In a filing on Wednesday, Incyte said that it agreed to pay Novartis $280M as part of the disputed royalties on U.S. sales of Jakafi. The companies also settled to reduce by 50% the royalty rate payable by Incyte on future sales of Jakafi in the U.S. beginning January 1, 2025.

Incyte said that this would lead to reduced royalty payable for the quarter ended March 31, 2025, to about $14.9M. 

The difference of $242.2M between the total accrued royalties and the total amount to be paid to Novartis, will be recorded in Incyte's results for the quarter ending June 30, 2025.

data.title: CAE targets 150% cash conversion and mid to high single digit Civil operating income growth for 2026 amid record $20.1B backlog
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CAE Inc. (CAE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Marc Parent, President and CEO, stated that "We delivered an exceptional fourth quarter that capped a strong year across all of our key financial and operational metrics," highlighting $289 million in free cash flow for the quarter and a record $814 million for the full year, with a cash conversion rate of 211%. He emphasized that this enabled CAE to meet its year-end leverage target and provided increased flexibility for future growth.

  • Parent reported building momentum for long-term growth and profitability, securing $1.3 billion in new orders in the quarter and ending the year with a record adjusted backlog of $20.1 billion, up 65% from last year. Civil segment backlog grew 37% to a record $8.8 billion, supported by $3.7 billion in new orders, including 56 full flight simulators.

  • Parent noted that Defense segment adjusted backlog nearly doubled to $11.3 billion, with $596 million in quarterly orders and a record $4 billion for the year. He cited strong program execution and market tailwinds as key drivers.

  • Constantino Malatesta, Interim CFO, stated, "Revenue of $1.3 billion was up 13% compared to the fourth quarter last year. Adjusted segment operating income was $258.8 million compared to $125.7 million last year. Quarterly EPS was $0.47 per share compared to $0.12 in the fourth quarter last year."

  • Malatesta added, "Net cash provided by operating activities was $322.7 million for the quarter compared to $216.2 million in the fourth quarter of last year. For the year, we generated $896.5 million from operating activities compared to $566.9 million last year."

  • Parent also announced a planned CEO transition later in the summer, with the Board in the process of identifying the next CEO and committing to a seamless handover.

Outlook

  • Parent stated, "We expect segment operating income to grow in the mid to high single digit percentage range, with a modest increase in the annual adjusted segment operating income margin" for Civil in fiscal 2026. He also indicated that the Defense segment is expected to have low double digit percentage annual segment operating income growth and an annual segment operating margin in the 8% to 8.5% range.

  • Management expects a cash conversion rate of 150% for fiscal 2026 and beyond, representing a significant increase from the previous 100% target.

  • Regarding capital expenditures, Malatesta said, "We remain highly focused on capital efficiency and expect total CapEx in fiscal 2026 to be modestly lower than in fiscal 2025."

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenue was $1.3 billion for the quarter, up 13% year-over-year. Adjusted segment operating income was $258.8 million, and EPS was $0.47 per share. For the full year, consolidated revenue was $4.7 billion, and adjusted segment operating income was $732 million. Annual adjusted net income was $385.5 million or $1.21 per share.

  • Free cash flow for the quarter was $289 million, a record $814 million for the year, and net cash provided by operating activities for the quarter was $322.7 million.

  • Civil fourth-quarter revenue was $728.4 million, and adjusted segment operating income was $208.4 million, with a margin of 28.6%. Civil delivered 15 full flight simulators in the quarter and 61 for the year. Defense fourth-quarter revenue was $547 million, and adjusted segment operating income was $50.4 million, with a 9.2% margin.

  • Net debt at the end of the quarter was $3.2 billion, with a net debt to adjusted EBITDA of 2.77 times.

Q&A

  • Kevin Chiang, CIBC: Asked about the Civil outlook, commercial versus business jet training demand, and margin outlook. Parent responded that CAE is taking a measured approach due to a "more cautious tone that we hear from airlines and a bit of softness that we saw late in fiscal year," noting resilience in training demand and expecting modestly lower simulator deliveries in the first half of the year.

  • Fadi Chamoun, BMO: Inquired about the flight operation business and competitive position. Parent replied, "We're winning in the market... our order book is growing. I can tell you that basically, I would say we're at capacity for the work that we can take on right now."

  • Konark Gupta, Scotiabank: Sought clarity on defense margin guidance. Parent explained, "We're not putting a lid on anything... we're ramping up higher margin programs that we won in committed backlog." Malatesta added, "We are aiming to take our leverage down to 2.5 times by the end of the year."

  • Cameron Doerksen, National Bank Financial: Asked about CapEx, with Malatesta noting, "Total CapEx expected to be modestly lower than last year... 25% maintenance CapEx." Parent emphasized the shift from a multiyear investment cycle to "consolidating and operationalizing that opportunity."

  • Greg Konrad, Jefferies: Queried Civil backlog conversion. Andrew Arnovitz indicated that consolidation of the SimCom JV and Flexjet contract, which is spread over 15 years, impacts year-one revenue recognition.

  • Tim James, TD Cowen: Asked about Canadian defense opportunities. Parent stated, "I feel very, very good that, that's going to translate into significant growth for CAE on top of the already very significant level of order intake and actually revenue that we're generating right now from contracts in Canada."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts adopted a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on margin outlooks, backlog conversion, and the strategic position in both Civil and Defense. Questions were probing but did not reflect heightened skepticism.

  • Management's tone was confident and optimistic in prepared remarks, with Parent stating, "With continued focus on execution, innovation and value creation, I'm confident that we'll build on this foundation to deliver growth, higher margins and stronger free cash flow." During Q&A, responses were more measured and occasionally cautious, reflecting careful optimism amid macro uncertainty.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a similar level of confidence but provided more specific guidance and acknowledged first-half caution in Civil.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Civil segment operating income shifted from "modestly below our previous outlook" last quarter to "mid to high single digit percentage range" growth this quarter, with a "modest increase" in margin.

  • Defense margin guidance evolved from "modestly above previously indicated range of 6% to 7%" to "8% to 8.5%" for the year ahead.

  • Management's tone remained confident, but with greater detail on cash conversion targets and capital allocation discipline. Analysts' focus shifted toward backlog conversion, margin sustainability, and capital efficiency.

  • Strategic focus moved further towards capital efficiency, deleveraging, and operationalizing growth investments, with less emphasis on new capacity expansion.

Risks and Concerns

  • Malatesta noted higher finance expense resulting from lease expenses and SIMCOM joint venture consolidation. Income tax expense increased, with an effective tax rate of 25% for the quarter versus 14% last year.

  • Parent discussed ongoing OEM supply chain constraints impacting aircraft deliveries and simulator demand, with expectations for "modestly lower simulator deliveries this fiscal year with a greater proportion occurring in the second half."

  • Analysts raised concerns about the timing of backlog conversion, margin sustainability in Defense, and macroeconomic uncertainty in Civil.

Final Takeaway

CAE enters fiscal 2026 with strong momentum, a record $20.1 billion backlog, and clear targets for growth in both Civil and Defense segments. Management projects mid to high single digit Civil operating income growth and low double digit Defense growth, with a focus on higher cash conversion, capital efficiency, and further deleveraging. Strategic priorities are centered on operational execution, resilience in core markets, and disciplined use of capital, positioning CAE for continued expansion and value creation for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, and Dow continue to rally after softer inflation report
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages rose on Wednesday, a day after the S&P and the Nasdaq closed higher, as soft inflation data soothed sentiments.

Early on and the S&P 500 (SP500) was +0.2%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.4%, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.2%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 1 basis point to 4.49%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) was flat at 4.02%.

Wall Street's benchmark S&P 500 index and tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite gauge on Tuesday extended a massive rally from the previous session, as sentiment was boosted by soft inflation data. The blue-chip Dow bucked the trend, weighed down by a plunge in component UnitedHealth.

“It's been another 24 hours where the Trump administration continues to hog the headlines. With the president in the Middle East, various stories on AI supported a huge tech-led rally...that got a further boost thanks to a softer-than-expected CPI print,” Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

On Tuesday, the headline and core consumer prices rose +0.2% M/M, below the +0.3% consensus. On an annual basis, headline CPI came in at +2.3%, also below the +2.4% estimate, while core CPI was in line at +2.8%. 

“Our U.S. economists think the April data is still too early for the Liberation Day tariffs to show up in the aggregate numbers, and they don’t expect the effects to show up in consumer prices until June,” Reid added.

U.S. consumer price inflation did not show many trade tax consequences. The slowing of tourism-related prices might reflect how visitors are wary of entering the U.S., but complications in timing Easter’s effect are also relevant. Appliance price inflation is a concern, UBS' Paul Donovan said.

The economic calendar is light today. The wholesale inflation data is expected to land on Thursday.

data.title: BofA Securities: Record Private Clients Buying Streak Since 2008
data.description:
BofA clients bought around $3.2 billion in U.S. equities for a third consecutive week last week, with private clients buying stocks in all sectors as well as ETFs.

Private clients have been buyers for 22 weeks straight, which is the longest buying streak in its data history since ’08. On the other hand, institutional clients were sellers for the first time in three weeks.

“This was the first week of net buying by hedge funds in five weeks (and only the third week of buying YTD) – cumulative sales by hedge funds YTD are the largest of any comparable period in our data since ’08,” said Jill Carey Hall, BofA Securities equity and quant strategist.

CFTC data have similarly suggested net short positions by speculative traders all year, potentially suggesting a pain trade for hedge funds if the rally continues, said Hall in a note.

The Consumer Discretionary (NYSEARCA:XLY) sector led the inflows, as clients bought stocks in eight sectors, including technology (NYSEARCA:XLF) and financials (NYSEARCA:XLF).

On the other hand, Materials sector (NYSEARCA:XLB) had their first week of outflows in 11 weeks, breaking the longest recent buying streak of the sectors, while communication (NYSEARCA:XLC) and industrials (NYSEARCA:XLI) also saw outflows.

“Clients bought ETFs in six sectors, led by financials and industrials. Energy (NYSEARCA:XLE) ETFs – which have seen the biggest outflows YTD – saw their first inflows in four weeks,” noted Hall in the weekly report.

data.title: Valeura Energy reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Valeura Energy press release (OTCQX:VLERF): Q1 Revenue of $148.08M (-0.9% Y/Y).

  • Oil production of 23,853 bbls/d, an increase of 9% compared to Q1 last year.

  • Adjusted opex trending downward, to US$24.1/bbl, a decrease of 8% compared to Q1 last year.

  • Adjusted Cashflow from Operations of US$74.0 million, an increase of 55% compared to Q1 2024, demonstrating the effects of the corporate restructuring and application of tax loss carry-forwards.

  • The Company's balance sheet remains very strong, with US$239 million cash and no debt; and

  • Adjusted Working Capital of US$254 million.

  • Operations Update and Outlook

    During Q1 2025, Valeura had ongoing production operations at all of its Gulf of Thailand fields, including Jasmine, Manora, Nong Yao, and Wassana fields. Total working interest share production before royalties averaged 23,853 bbls/d, which was in line with management's expectations and consistent with achieving the Company's guidance range for the full year 2025 of 23,000 - 25,500 bbls/d. One drilling rig was under contract throughout the quarter.

data.title: 4 stocks to watch on Wednesday: CSCO, CRWV, SBUX and KHC
data.description:
U.S. stock index futures on Wednesday were slightly higher, following two straight days of strong sentiment. Here are some stocks to watch on Wednesday: 

  • Shares of Cisco (NASDAQ:CSCO) will be in focus, ahead of the networking giant's FQ3 2025 results after the closing bell. Wall Street expects the firm to earn 92 cents per share on revenue of $14.06B. The top-line figure is seen rising 11% Y/Y, as enterprise software continues to recover. Investors will also be looking at Cisco's (NASDAQ:CSCO) artificial intelligence-related offerings. The company on Tuesday unveiled a new partnership with Saudi Arabia's newly formed AI company, Humain AI.
  • CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) stock will garner attention, ahead of the AI hyperscaler's first quarterly results as a public company. Wall Street expects the Livingston, N.J.-based firm to lose 12 cents per share on revenue of $859.77M. According to Macquarie, CoreWeave's AI customizability, along with its highly efficient platform has made it a disruptor in an industry historically dominated by major general-purpose cloud computing platforms.
  • Shares of Starbucks (NASDAQ:SBUX) were -0.2% in pre-market trading. The world's largest coffee chain is facing a fresh round of strikes by baristas in more than 50 stores. The employees have taken issue with Starbucks' (NASDAQ:SBUX) new dress code policy, which requires solid black tops and blue, black, or khaki bottoms. Many baristas argue that the policy imposes an unfair financial burden, as they are only provided with two company shirts and must purchase additional clothing themselves.
  • Kraft Heinz (NASDAQ:KHC) stock was flat ahead of the opening bell. Reuters reported that the ketchup maker will be spending $3B to upgrade its U.S. factories, citing an interview with Pedro Navio, KHC's president of North America. This would be the company's largest investment in its plants in a decade, even as it struggles with soft sales, low consumer demand and impacts of tariffs. The upgrades will help lower costs by making the plants more efficient, Reuters said.
